stem: A hospital uses a blood screening test to screen for a rare genetic condition. The test has a sensitivity of 94% and a specificity of 97%. The prevalence of a rare genetic condition in the patient population is estimated to be between 0.6% and 1.7%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has a rare genetic condition? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(a rare genetic condition | positive test) ranges from 0.159 to 0.351 as prevalence varies from 0.6% to 1.7%. Point estimate at 1.1% prevalence: 0.258.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.03.
The confidence interval [0.159, 0.351] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (94%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
